file_name: BOLIVARMININGCORP_05_23_2003-EX-2.1-VISP WEB SITE BUILDING AND HOSTING AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: All questions regarding the validity, interpretation, performance and enforcement of the provisions of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the state of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: All questions regarding the interpretation of this Agreement shall be subject to non-binding mediation in a location determined by the Provider, but the validity, performance and enforcement shall be governed by the laws of the state of New York.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that interpretation is subject to non-binding mediation somewhere the Provider chooses, it creates an ambiguity that circumvents New York law, which should govern all aspects of the agreement, including interpretation. This creates a loophole, allowing the Provider to influence the interpretation outside the jurisdiction's legal framework.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 states that agreements containing a choice of New York law are enforceable, provided the agreement involves at least $250,000. The modification attempts to introduce an out-of-state, non-binding mediation for interpretation, which undermines the intent of New York law to govern all aspects of the contract, including its interpretation and enforcement within the state's legal system.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: State Government Sites State Government Sites Alaska Case Law Service Arizona Court Rules Arkansas Model Jury Instructions - Civil Arkansas Regulations California Code of Regulations California Public Law Library Briefs Service Clarence Darrow Cases Iowa Department of Justice, Office of the Attorney General Opinions Kentucky Court Rules Maryland Code and Court Rules Michigan Official Reports Mississippi Attorney General Opinions New York Official Reports Service New York State Register Official Oklahoma Session Laws Official Oklahoma Statutes (Unannotated) Oklahoma Historical Session Laws Oklahoma Historical Statutes (Unannotated) Small Business Administration Office Unofficial New York Codes, Rules and Regulations Unofficial Purdon's Pennsylvania Statutes from Westlaw Virginia Attorney General Opinions Washington Civil Jury Instructions Washington Criminal Jury Instructions Washington Pattern Jury Instructions Washington State Employment Security Department Precedential Decisions of Commissioner
perturbation.justification: The original text states that New York law governs all aspects of the agreement. The changed text introduces non-binding mediation for interpretation, potentially outside of New York, creating an ambiguity. The Perturbation Explanation highlights this circumvention of New York law. The Law Explanation mentions that New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 aims to enforce agreements with a choice of New York law, suggesting the modification undermines this intent by allowing interpretation to occur outside the state's legal framework. Scraped Law Snippet 2 also indicates New York has official reports and codes, which could be circumvented. The introduction of non-binding mediation and the Provider's choice of location for this mediation introduces ambiguity that undermines the original intent to adhere to New York Law, thus contradicting the agreement's original legal alignment.

perturbation.original_text: KCI shall promptly and fully cooperate with Provider to address and resolve all issues, problems, administrative procedures, End User complaints, regulatory investigations or inquiries or any other circumstances arising from KCI's use of Provider's services.
perturbation.changed_text: KCI shall endeavor to cooperate with Provider to address issues related to KCI's use of Provider's services to a degree deemed reasonable by KCI's internal policies.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall promptly and fully cooperate' with 'shall endeavor to cooperate...to a degree deemed reasonable by KCI's internal policies' introduces significant ambiguity. The level of cooperation is now subjective and dictated by KCI's internal policies, potentially allowing KCI to avoid full cooperation, especially during regulatory investigations. This weakens Provider's ability to resolve issues or ensure KCI's compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Executive Law § 63(12) empowers the Attorney General to investigate and prosecute fraudulent or illegal activities. The modified clause, limiting KCI's cooperation, could hinder the Attorney General's ability to investigate potential fraud or illegal activities related to the Provider's services, contradicting the state's mandate for full cooperation during such investigations. Full cooperation is replaced by limited cooperation, according to KCI's policies.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'promptly and fully cooperate,' while the modified version only requires KCI to 'endeavor to cooperate...to a degree deemed reasonable by KCI's internal policies.' This introduces ambiguity, potentially allowing KCI to limit cooperation during investigations. Although there are no scraped law snippets, the Perturbation Explanation indicates that this change weakens the Provider's ability to ensure KCI's compliance. The Law Explanation suggests that this could hinder investigations by the Attorney General. Therefore, the altered text contradicts the expectation of full cooperation that could be expected during investigations, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be for a term of five years from the date of this document. This agreement shall renew automatically each year thereafter, unless either party serves written notice of its intention not to renew, on the other at least 90 days prior to the expiration of the then current term of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be for a term of five years from the date of this document, unless unforeseen circumstances arise. Renewal will be discussed annually, with both parties expressing their intentions, though renewal is not guaranteed.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause clearly defines the term and renewal process. The modified clause introduces 'unforeseen circumstances' as a potential reason for early termination and removes the automatic renewal, replacing it with a vague discussion where renewal is 'not guaranteed'. This creates ambiguity and makes the duration of the agreement uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York UCC § 2-309 addresses the absence of specific time provisions in contracts. The modified clause introducing 'unforeseen circumstances' and removing guaranteed renewal creates uncertainty about the contract's duration, potentially allowing a party to terminate without reasonable notification. This contradicts the UCC's implied obligation of reasonable duration and notification for termination.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-309 Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 3 Section 2--309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity regarding the contract's duration and termination, contradicting UCC § 2-309. The original text clearly defines a five-year term with automatic renewal, subject to a 90-day notice. The changed text replaces this with 'unforeseen circumstances' for early termination and removes the guaranteed renewal, stating that renewal will be 'discussed annually' but is 'not guaranteed'. This directly contradicts the requirement in UCC § 2-309(3) that 'Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party'. The modified clause allows for termination based on 'unforeseen circumstances' without guaranteeing reasonable notification, creating a legal contradiction as it undermines the predictability and notification requirements for contract termination.
